title: Human lineage and ape ancestry: a cautious starter dataset

scope.oldest: Possible early hominoids near the split of apes and Old World monkeys, about 25 Ma
scope.focus: Candidate populations on the human line, informative sister lineages, representative fossils, changes, and testable explanations
scope.notAClaim: The ordered records are not a proven chain of direct ancestors. Species are samples from branching populations, and the fossil record is too incomplete to identify most direct ancestors.

interpretationRules: A fossil documents an organism, not automatically an ancestor.
Observed anatomy and inferred selective pressure are separate fields.
A life reconstruction is an interpretation; skin, hair, fat and much behavior usually do not fossilize.
Date ranges are estimates and can change with new dating or taxonomic assignments.
The genus Homo and especially Middle Pleistocene Homo have disputed species boundaries.
Evolution branches and populations can exchange genes; it is not a ladder of progress.

divergenceEvents.label: Separation of the line leading to Homo sapiens from Neanderthal–Denisovan populations
divergenceEvents.note: A population split, not an instantaneous clean break; later gene flow occurred.

divergenceEvents.label: Last common ancestral population of humans and chimpanzees/bonobos
divergenceEvents.note: No named fossil species is securely identified as this ancestor. Sahelanthropus and Orrorin fall near this interval but their exact positions remain debated.

divergenceEvents.label: Last common ancestral population of humans, chimpanzees/bonobos and gorillas
divergenceEvents.basis: genome divergence models and sparse fossils
divergenceEvents.note: Nakalipithecus is useful context near this interval, not a demonstrated direct ancestor.

divergenceEvents.label: Last common ancestral population of living great apes
divergenceEvents.note: This is the point before the orangutan line separated from the African-ape line.

divergenceEvents.label: Last common ancestral population of all living apes
divergenceEvents.basis: genome divergence models and early Miocene hominoids
divergenceEvents.note: This is the requested deep endpoint. Ekembo is an informative early hominoid, but is not proven to be the crown-ape ancestor.

divergenceEvents.label: Minimum fossil calibration for the ape–Old World monkey split
divergenceEvents.note: This lies just beyond the requested endpoint and anchors the deeper context.

taxa.summary: Our species evolved in Africa from structured and connected Middle Pleistocene populations; it was not born at one sharp instant or single known site.
observedTraits: reduced brow ridges and face relative to many earlier Homo
lightly built postcranial skeleton on average
taxa.changeSummary: Early specimens combine a modern-looking face with a more elongated braincase; later populations show the more globular vault typical today.
whyHypotheses: Large, connected and periodically fragmented African populations may have assembled modern traits in a mosaic pattern.
Behavioral flexibility and social learning likely mattered, but no single climatic or cognitive trigger is demonstrated.
taxa.relationshipCertainty: high for species reality; low for any single immediate fossil ancestor

taxa.summary: A close sister lineage, not a primitive stage of Homo sapiens. Neanderthals interbred with ancestors of many living people.
taxa.changeSummary: Compared with the shared ancestral population, Neanderthal anatomy became highly robust and distinctive in Pleistocene Eurasia.
whyHypotheses: Body proportions are consistent with heat conservation in cold climates.
Drift and repeated small-population bottlenecks also shaped distinctive anatomy; climate is not a complete explanation.

regions: genetic legacy across Asia and Oceania
taxa.summary: An archaic human population first recognized from DNA, with very sparse securely attributed anatomy and no universally accepted formal species name.
observedTraits: very large molars in some attributed fossils
genetically distinct sister group to Neanderthals
taxa.changeSummary: The anatomical story remains fragmentary; genomic evidence is currently much richer than the fossil evidence.
whyHypotheses: Multiple Asian environments and long population separation likely produced regional adaptations.
Specific life reconstructions remain unusually speculative because no complete skull is securely known.
taxa.relationshipCertainty: high genomic identity; medium or low for many fossil attributions

taxa.scientificName: Middle Pleistocene Homo (often grouped as Homo heidelbergensis)
taxa.summary: A deliberately broad placeholder for diverse Middle Pleistocene populations. Applying Homo heidelbergensis to all of them can hide real regional structure.
observedTraits: larger brains than most Homo erectus
massive brow ridges in many specimens
taxa.changeSummary: Brain size and cranial differentiation increased while African and Eurasian populations became more regionally structured.
whyHypotheses: Variable climates, expanded social foraging and demographic separation are plausible contributors.
The taxonomic grouping is too coarse to assign one pressure to all included populations.
taxa.relationshipCertainty: medium at population-grade level; low for the traditional all-inclusive species

taxa.summary: The first hominin with broadly modern human-like body proportions and the first well-supported large dispersal beyond Africa.
observedTraits: long legs and shorter arms relative to torso
larger body and brain than earlier Homo on average
thick cranial bone and strong brow ridge
association with Acheulean tools in much of its range
taxa.changeSummary: A major shift toward a larger, more ranging terrestrial body accompanied by an expanding but still low, long braincase.
whyHypotheses: Larger ranges, dietary flexibility and access to energy-dense foods may have supported bigger bodies and dispersal.
Environmental variability is a plausible filter, not a proven single cause.
Fire use is patchy and should not be presented as universal for the species.
taxa.relationshipCertainty: high as a broad fossil grade; medium for its exact species boundaries and direct ancestry

taxa.summary: A variable early Homo species with a larger brain and smaller face and teeth than australopiths on average, while retaining many climbing-related traits.
observedTraits: mosaic of Homo-like teeth and brain with australopith-like body traits
taxa.changeSummary: Cranial and dental change preceded a fully modern human-like body plan.
whyHypotheses: A broad omnivorous diet and flexible tool-assisted foraging may have favored this mosaic.
Oldowan tools cannot be assigned exclusively to Homo habilis because several hominins overlapped.
taxa.relationshipCertainty: medium as a species; low to medium as a direct Homo erectus ancestor

taxa.summary: A long-lived collateral branch that evolved an extreme chewing apparatus while overlapping early Homo.
taxa.changeSummary: Chewing-force anatomy became far more specialized than in contemporary Homo.
whyHypotheses: Dental microwear and isotopes complicate the old 'hard-object specialist' story.
The apparatus may have helped process abundant tough C4 foods or fallback foods rather than only nuts and seeds.
taxa.relationshipCertainty: high as a collateral lineage; not considered a human ancestor

taxa.summary: A well-sampled bipedal australopith that retained strong climbing abilities and is close in time and anatomy to the ancestry of later australopiths and Homo.
observedTraits: valgus knee and human-like heel-strike evidence
taxa.changeSummary: Bipedal walking was established while arboreal capability remained important; brain expansion had not yet occurred.
whyHypotheses: Mosaic woodland–grassland environments made flexible ground and tree use valuable.
No single savanna event explains bipedality; locomotion evolved before large brains and before open grasslands dominated many sites.
taxa.relationshipCertainty: high as a species; medium as a source population near later lineages

taxa.summary: The oldest generally accepted Australopithecus; legs show bipedality while jaws and teeth retain primitive features.
taxa.changeSummary: More committed terrestrial bipedality appears alongside a still primitive skull and jaw.
whyHypotheses: Woodland and gallery-forest settings near water argue against a simple open-savanna cause.
Flexible access to both trees and ground resources is a better-supported ecological context than one exclusive pressure.
taxa.relationshipCertainty: high as a species; medium for direct transition to A. afarensis because the species overlapped in time

taxa.summary: A woodland hominin with an unusual locomotor mosaic: capable of upright walking on the ground but retaining a grasping big toe and arboreal adaptations.
observedTraits: short broad upper pelvis compatible with bipedality
taxa.changeSummary: Early bipedal traits combined with a foot unlike later hominins, showing that bipedality did not arrive as one finished package.
whyHypotheses: Woodland evidence rejects the claim that bipedality required treeless savanna.
Canine reduction has been linked to social behavior, but that causal interpretation is difficult to test.
taxa.relationshipCertainty: high as a species; medium as an early hominin; low as a proven direct ancestor

taxa.summary: A sparse collection of teeth and postcranial fragments near the human–chimp divergence interval.
observedTraits: more projecting canines than A. ramidus
reduced canine honing relative to apes
toe-bone morphology interpreted as compatible with bipedality
taxa.changeSummary: Dental reduction and possible bipedality appear, but the sample is too sparse for a whole-body reconstruction.
whyHypotheses: Dietary and social explanations for canine change remain hypotheses.
Locomotor conclusions rest on limited material and should be shown with low confidence.
taxa.relationshipCertainty: medium as a taxon; low to medium as a direct ancestor

taxa.summary: A fragmentary late Miocene ape whose femur combines signals associated with bipedality and climbing.
observedTraits: proximal femur consistent with some habitual bipedal loading
strong climbing-related arm and finger traits
taxa.changeSummary: Possible ground bipedality coexisted with substantial arboreal locomotion.
whyHypotheses: A mixed woodland setting could reward locomotor flexibility.
Its femur does not by itself prove that Orrorin lies on the human rather than another ape line.
taxa.relationshipCertainty: medium for bipedal signal; low to medium for phylogenetic position

taxa.summary: One of the oldest proposed hominins, known from a distorted skull, jaws and postcranial bones far west of the East African Rift.
taxa.changeSummary: A possible early combination of reduced canines, bipedality and continued tree use.
whyHypotheses: The associated fauna indicate a mosaic near a lake, not a simple arid savanna.
Cranial distortion and uncertain association of postcranial bones keep locomotion and ancestry debated.
taxa.relationshipCertainty: medium as a species; debated as a hominin and direct ancestor

taxa.summary: A late Miocene great ape from Africa close in time to estimates for African-ape diversification, but known mainly from jaws and teeth.
taxa.changeSummary: It fills a major African fossil gap rather than documenting a known step toward humans.
whyHypotheses: Its presence supports continued African great-ape diversity near the gorilla and human–chimp branching interval.
Sparse anatomy prevents a reliable life appearance or locomotor reconstruction.
taxa.relationshipCertainty: low as a direct ancestor; medium as relevant geographic and temporal context

taxa.summary: A well-known early Miocene hominoid formerly placed in Proconsul, useful for showing the body plan near early ape diversification.
observedTraits: no suspensory specializations of living apes
taxa.changeSummary: Shows that early apes lacked the full locomotor packages of any living ape group.
whyHypotheses: Changes in forest structure and feeding ecology likely influenced early hominoid diversification.
It should not be drawn as a chimp-like direct ancestor; living apes are each specialized.
taxa.relationshipCertainty: high as an early hominoid; low as a member of the crown-ape ancestor line

taxa.summary: A partial jaw with teeth interpreted as an early hominoid, providing a fossil minimum near the ape–Old World monkey split.
observedTraits: known only from limited jaw material
taxa.changeSummary: Anchors the existence of the ape lineage by the late Oligocene; it cannot support a whole-body evolutionary story alone.
whyHypotheses: Its importance is chronological and phylogenetic rather than evidence for a particular adaptation.
taxa.relationshipCertainty: medium as a hominoid; low as a direct ancestor of living apes

relationships.note: Older possible hominoid, not a demonstrated crown-ape ancestor.
Samples an early ape body plan near the estimated crown-ape interval.
Plausibly near African great-ape ancestry but too incomplete for a direct claim.
Age and anatomy are compatible, but placement remains debated.
Femoral evidence supports some bipedality; direct ancestry is not established.
Chronology and dental change fit, but fossils do not demonstrate direct population continuity.
A substantial fossil and temporal gap remains.
Often treated as anagenesis, but temporal overlap allows branching populations.
The transition to early Homo is poorly sampled and may involve other australopith taxa.
Represents the robust australopith branch, not a human ancestor.
They overlapped for hundreds of thousands of years, so a simple whole-species replacement is misleading.
Some geographically structured Homo erectus populations likely contributed to later Homo diversity.
African Middle Pleistocene populations, not necessarily every fossil called H. heidelbergensis, contributed to H. sapiens ancestry.
European Middle Pleistocene populations developed increasingly Neanderthal-like traits.
Genomes place them as sister groups after their shared line split from the H. sapiens line.
Later interbreeding contributed Neanderthal ancestry to many living non-African populations.
Multiple episodes contributed Denisovan ancestry to some living Asian and Oceanian populations.

fossils.material: crania, mandible, teeth and postcrania from several individuals
fossils.importance: Early H. sapiens facial morphology with a less globular braincase.
One of the oldest fossils with a strongly modern cranial form; minimum age constrained by volcanic ash.
Substantial healed injuries provide evidence of survival with impairment.
Yielded the high-coverage genome that defined Denisovans.
Protein evidence links the fossil to Denisovans and shows high-altitude presence by about 160 ka.
A robust African Middle Pleistocene skull traditionally assigned to H. heidelbergensis or H. rhodesiensis.
Best whole-body evidence for long legs, narrow trunk and terrestrial body proportions.
Early dispersal and extreme variation within one local Homo population.
Youngest well-dated surviving H. erectus population.

fossils.material: juvenile mandible, teeth and hand bones
fossils.importance: Type specimen; digital reconstruction altered estimates of early Homo diversity and brain size.
Classic evidence for the extreme chewing anatomy of P. boisei.
Combines bipedal pelvis and knee with climbing-related upper-body traits.
Direct behavioral evidence of habitual bipedal walking; attribution to A. afarensis is strong but not literally proven by bones in the tracks.
Reveals the face of A. anamensis and indicates temporal overlap with A. afarensis.

fossils.material: fragmentary skull, teeth, pelvis, limbs, hands and feet
fossils.importance: Rare whole-body evidence for a locomotor mosaic unlike living chimpanzees.

fossils.specimen: ALA-VP-2/10 and associated Middle Awash material
fossils.material: teeth and fragmentary postcrania including a toe bone
fossils.importance: Sparse evidence for canine change and possible bipedality.
Key evidence for bipedal loading in Orrorin.
Reduced canines and cranial-base orientation near the human–chimp split interval.

fossils.material: partial left femur and two ulnae
fossils.importance: Postcranial evidence interpreted as habitual bipedality combined with arboreal behavior; association and interpretation have been debated.
Rare African great-ape evidence near estimated African-ape divergences.

fossils.material: multiple skulls, teeth and partial skeletons
fossils.importance: Unusually informative early hominoid body evidence.
Possible oldest crownward hominoid fossil and a minimum calibration for the ape lineage.

adaptationStories.label: Bipedality evolved as a mosaic
adaptationStories.observedChange: Femora, pelves, knees, feet and trackways progressively document upright loading and habitual walking while climbing traits persisted.
adaptationStories.evidenceAssessment: Anatomical change is strong; no single proposed pressure uniquely explains it. Woodland Ardipithecus falsifies an exclusively open-savanna trigger.
adaptationStories.confidence: high for change; low for a single cause

adaptationStories.label: Reduced canine honing
adaptationStories.observedChange: Upper canines became smaller and stopped sharpening against the lower premolar compared with most living apes.
candidatePressures: changes in male competition and social signaling
adaptationStories.evidenceAssessment: The dental trend is observable; social-system explanations are difficult to test from fossils and should remain labeled hypotheses.
adaptationStories.confidence: high for change; low for cause

adaptationStories.label: A body for terrestrial ranging
adaptationStories.observedChange: Homo erectus had longer legs, shorter arms relative to its torso and a larger body than australopiths and early Homo on average.
candidatePressures: larger daily and seasonal foraging ranges
adaptationStories.evidenceAssessment: The body change is strong. Energetics and range explanations fit several data streams but cannot be reduced to one cause.
adaptationStories.confidence: high for change; medium for combined ecological explanation

adaptationStories.label: Parallel food-processing strategies
adaptationStories.observedChange: Paranthropus expanded jaws and cheek teeth while Homo reduced teeth and increasingly relied on hands, tools and flexible food acquisition.
adaptationStories.evidenceAssessment: Anatomy and isotopes support divergent diets, but tools at shared sites usually cannot be assigned to one maker.
adaptationStories.confidence: high for anatomical divergence; medium for niche explanation

adaptationStories.label: Brain expansion was uneven and energetically costly
adaptationStories.observedChange: Average endocranial volume increased across Homo, but with overlap, reversals and large variation rather than a smooth march.
adaptationStories.evidenceAssessment: The long-term trend is real; proposed feedbacks among diet, culture and sociality are difficult to separate and should not be presented as a single-cause story.
adaptationStories.confidence: high for broad trend; low to medium for specific causes

adaptationStories.label: Branches rejoined through gene flow
adaptationStories.observedChange: Genomes demonstrate interbreeding among Homo sapiens, Neanderthals and Denisovans after long periods of population separation.
candidatePressures: range expansions and contact during shifting Pleistocene climates
adaptationStories.evidenceAssessment: Gene flow is strongly supported; the ecological causes and frequency of contact are less certain.
adaptationStories.confidence: high for gene flow; medium for climatic-contact explanation

visualAssets.title: Composite reconstruction of Jebel Irhoud fossils
visualAssets.creator: Philipp Gunz, MPI EVA Leipzig
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: A reconstruction of fossil cranial material, not a color life restoration.

visualAssets.title: Neanderthal reconstruction at the Natural History Museum, London
visualAssets.creator: Werner Ustorf; museum model artist not stated in Commons metadata
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: Photograph is licensed; soft tissue, hair and pigmentation are interpretive.

visualAssets.title: Forensic reconstruction labeled Homo heidelbergensis
visualAssets.creator: Cicero Moraes
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: The taxon is disputed and a face cannot represent all Middle Pleistocene Homo populations.

visualAssets.title: Nariokotome Boy reconstruction at Neanderthal Museum
visualAssets.creator: Neanderthal Museum
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: Body proportions are fossil-based; hair, skin and facial soft tissues are interpretive.

visualAssets.title: Homo habilis skull reconstructions in a museum display
visualAssets.creator: Tiia Monto
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: These are reconstructed skull casts, not original fossils or a life restoration.

visualAssets.title: Paranthropus boisei reconstruction at Smithsonian NMNH
visualAssets.creator: Ryan Schwark (photograph); model artist not stated in Commons metadata
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: Chewing anatomy is fossil-based; soft tissue, hair and pigmentation are interpretive.

visualAssets.title: Australopithecus afarensis reconstructed skull cast
visualAssets.creator: Durova
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: A reconstructed skull cast, not an original fossil or a life restoration.

visualAssets.title: Reconstructed Ardipithecus ramidus hand bones at the Natural History Museum, London
visualAssets.creator: Bjoertvedt
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: A museum reconstruction of hand bones, not original fossils or a life restoration.

visualAssets.title: Homo habilis face illustration
visualAssets.creator: Artist not confirmed in page metadata
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: A face illustration extrapolated from incomplete fossils; pigmentation, hair and soft tissues are interpretive.

visualAssets.title: Australopithecus afarensis reconstruction based on AL 444-2
visualAssets.creator: John Gurche
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: Cranial proportions are fossil-based; pigmentation, hair and soft tissues are interpretive.

visualAssets.title: Ardipithecus ramidus face illustration
visualAssets.creator: Artist not confirmed in page metadata
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: A face illustration extrapolated from crushed and fragmentary cranial remains; most visible details are interpretive.

visualAssets.title: Sahelanthropus reconstruction by John Gurche
visualAssets.creator: John Gurche
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: The skull is distorted, no complete skeleton is known, and most visible soft tissue is interpretive.

visualAssets.title: Neanderthal composite skeleton
visualAssets.creator: Smithsonian Human Origins Program
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: A composite assembled from multiple Neanderthal individuals, used because no single complete skeleton exists.

visualAssets.title: Xiahe mandible
visualAssets.creator: Chen et al., Nature (2019)
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: The most complete securely attributed Denisovan fossil currently represented here; it does not constrain a whole face or body.

visualAssets.title: Kabwe 1 cranium
visualAssets.creator: Smithsonian Human Origins Program
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: A nearly complete cranium; the species label applied to Middle Pleistocene Homo remains disputed.

visualAssets.title: KNM-WT 15000 skeleton
visualAssets.creator: Smithsonian Human Origins Program
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: The most complete Homo erectus skeleton known; missing portions are reconstructed.

visualAssets.title: OH 5 cranium
visualAssets.creator: Smithsonian Human Origins Program
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: A well-preserved cranium showing the species' specialized chewing anatomy.

visualAssets.title: AL 288-1 (Lucy) skeleton
visualAssets.creator: Smithsonian Human Origins Program
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: About 40% of one skeleton; mirrored and reconstructed elements complete the display.

visualAssets.title: KNM-KP 29285 tibia
visualAssets.creator: Smithsonian Human Origins Program
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: A partial tibia; selected because it preserves strong evidence of habitual bipedality.

visualAssets.title: Ardipithecus ramidus partial skeleton reconstruction
visualAssets.creator: Tobias Fluegel
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: A composite display of the fragmentary Ardi skeleton, not a complete fossil body.

visualAssets.title: Generic early-hominin reconstruction
visualAssets.creator: Smithsonian Human Origins Program
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: A best-guess generic early-hominin illustration; Ardipithecus kadabba is too fragmentary for a species-specific face.

visualAssets.title: BAR 1002'00 femur
visualAssets.creator: Smithsonian Human Origins Program
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: The most informative postcranial fossil attributed to Orrorin; no complete body is known.

visualAssets.title: TM 266-01-060-1 (Toumaï) cranium
visualAssets.creator: Smithsonian Human Origins Program
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: A substantially complete but distorted cranium; reconstruction affects the apparent facial proportions.

visualAssets.title: Nakalipithecus mandible reconstruction
visualAssets.creator: Nikhil Iyengar
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: A jaw reconstruction based on the limited known mandibular and dental material.

visualAssets.title: Ekembo nyanzae skeleton reconstruction
visualAssets.creator: Ghedoghedo
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: A reconstructed museum skeleton assembled from known anatomy.

visualAssets.title: Rukwapithecus holotype in comparative figure
visualAssets.creator: Stevens et al., Nature (2013)
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: The taxon is represented by a partial jaw in a comparative plate; no whole-body fossil exists.

visualAssets.title: Full-body Neanderthal reconstruction
visualAssets.creator: Abraham (photograph); Henryk Fudali (sculpture)
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: A full-body artistic reconstruction; body proportions are informed by fossils while hair, skin and clothing remain interpretive.

visualAssets.title: Full-body Nariokotome Boy reconstruction
visualAssets.creator: Mauricio Antón
visualAssets.scientificCaveat: A full-body reconstruction based on KNM-WT 15000; missing soft tissue and body hair are interpretive.

coverageGaps.gap: No complete securely attributed skull or body; do not use a confident full-body reconstruction.
No vetted open-license color life reconstruction selected yet.
Material is too fragmentary for a taxon-specific whole-body restoration; Smithsonian uses a generic common-ancestor illustration.
No vetted open-license reconstruction selected; the fossil sample cannot determine most of its appearance.
Known mainly from jaws and teeth; a color whole-body depiction would be highly speculative.
Whole-body anatomy is relatively informative, but no vetted open-license color reconstruction was selected in this pass.
Known from a partial jaw; no defensible taxon-specific whole-body depiction.
